source.title: Screening for Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm: US Preventive Services Task Force Recommendation Statement

extraction.summary: The USPSTF recommends one-time ultrasonography screening for abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) in men aged 65-75 years who have ever smoked, and selective screening in men who never smoked. Screening is not recommended for women who never smoked and have no family history of AAA.

population.age_criteria: Adults 50 years or older, with evidence focused on ages 65-75 years
population.risk_criteria: Ever smokers (100+ cigarettes), family history of AAA in first-degree relative, male sex, older age
population.disease_definition: Abdominal aortic aneurysm typically defined as aortic enlargement with diameter ≥3.0 cm
population.exclusions: Patients unable to undergo surgical intervention or with reduced life expectancy due to comorbid conditions
population.severity_staging: Small AAAs: 3.0-5.4 cm; Large AAAs: ≥5.5 cm (surgical threshold for men); Women may need repair at 5.0-5.4 cm

key_thresholds.context: Lower threshold recommended by some guidelines

recommendations.recommendation: One-time screening with ultrasonography in men aged 65-75 years who have ever smoked
recommendations.evidence_level: Moderate certainty of moderate net benefit
recommendations.notes: Number needed to screen: 305 men to prevent 1 AAA death

recommendations.recommendation: Selectively offer screening to men aged 65-75 years who have never smoked
recommendations.evidence_level: Moderate certainty of small net benefit
recommendations.notes: Consider individual patient factors, medical history, family history, and personal values

recommendations.recommendation: Do not routinely screen women who have never smoked and have no family history of AAA
recommendations.evidence_level: Moderate certainty that harms outweigh benefits
recommendations.notes: Based on low prevalence and higher operative mortality in women

recommendations.recommendation: Insufficient evidence for screening women aged 65-75 years who have ever smoked or have family history of AAA
recommendations.evidence_level: Insufficient evidence to assess balance of benefits and harms
recommendations.notes: Evidence gap - need more research in this population

recommendations.recommendation: One-time screening with abdominal ultrasonography for men aged 65-75 years who have ever smoked
recommendations.strength: B (high certainty of moderate net benefit)
recommendations.evidence_level: High quality evidence from 4 randomized controlled trials
recommendations.notes: Pooled analysis showed statistically significant 35% reduction in AAA-related mortality

recommendations.recommendation: Selectively offer screening for men aged 65-75 years who have never smoked
recommendations.strength: C (moderate certainty of small net benefit)
recommendations.notes: Decision should be individualized based on patient context and values
Evidence insufficient to assess balance of benefits and harms in women

recommendations.recommendation: Emergency department bedside ultrasonography for diagnosing suspected abdominal aortic aneurysm
recommendations.notes: Referenced from academic emergency medicine literature

medications.class: Beta-blocker - high discontinuation rates due to adverse events (38-60%)

red_flags: AAA diameter ≥5.5 cm in men requiring immediate surgical referral
AAA diameter ≥5.0-5.4 cm in women (per some guidelines)
Rapid aneurysm growth (≥1.0 cm increase in diameter over 1 year)
Signs or symptoms of aneurysm rupture
Acute abdominal or back pain in patient with known AAA
extraction.follow_up: One-time screening only. For detected small AAAs (3.0-5.4 cm): ultrasound surveillance at regular intervals, though adherence reported as low as 65%
extraction.escalation_path: Positive screen with AAA ≥5.5 cm (men) or ≥5.0-5.4 cm (women per some guidelines): refer for surgical evaluation. Options include endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR) or open surgical repair. Small AAAs (3.0-5.4 cm): surveillance with periodic ultrasound monitoring.
